# Bayes for a continuous parameter

In the last module the answer was one of three reefs — a short list. Real
ecological questions are usually not like that.
**How long is the jaw of this frogfish species, on average?**
The answer is a number that could land *anywhere* on a continuous scale. The
Bayesian logic does not change at all; only the sum in the denominator turns into
an integral.

## A frogfish with a very big mouth

Frogfish are ambush predators. They sit camouflaged on the reef and strike by
opening an enormous mouth that swallows prey whole — so jaw size genuinely matters
for what a species can eat. Say you measure the lower jaw of seven individuals
from one species. Your measuring is a little noisy (a known measurement error, a
fixed standard deviation $\sigma$), and the readings scatter around some true
species mean $\mu$ that you would love to pin down.

That unknown mean $\mu$ is now a **continuous** parameter. It could be 38 mm, or
41.6 mm, or anything in between.

## The same theorem, continuous version

$$
p(\mu \mid y) = \frac{p(y \mid \mu)\,p(\mu)}{\displaystyle\int p(y \mid \mu)\,p(\mu)\,d\mu}
$$

It is the same recipe as before. The **likelihood** $p(y\mid\mu)$ is largest near
the average of your seven measurements — that is the value of $\mu$ that best
explains the readings. The **prior** $p(\mu)$ says what jaw lengths you found
plausible before measuring anything.

## Why the integral?

The denominator still adds up `prior × likelihood` over every value $\mu$ could
take. But now there are infinitely many such values, so you cannot list them and
add them one by one. Instead you **slice the $\mu$-axis into thin strips**, add up
`prior × likelihood × (strip width)`, and imagine the strips getting thinner and
thinner without end. That limiting sum *is* the integral. Same bookkeeping, finer
grid.

Here is a shortcut that saves a lot of effort. To find the **single most probable**
value — the **maximum a posteriori (MAP)** estimate — you do not need the
denominator at all. It is just a constant that does not depend on $\mu$, so the
peak of `prior × likelihood` sits at exactly the same place as the peak of the
full posterior.

## Your task

Drag the slider to the peak of the posterior (purple). Watch how the prior (cyan)
and likelihood (orange) combine into it. Then switch to a tighter prior and find
the new MAP — notice how a confident prior tugs the answer toward itself.
